steps.title: Neurology
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Secondary Care

steps.title: Acute Weakness
steps.text: What is the recommended treatment approach for schistosomiasis-related paraparesis when no other cause is identified?
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends Praziquantel 40 mg/kg STAT for all paraparesis cases, and a 3-day course may be considered when schistosomiasis is very likely.

textChoices.text: Start corticosteroids and monitor for 7 days
Give Praziquantel 40 mg/kg STAT or a 3-day course if highly probable
Refer immediately for surgical decompression
Withhold treatment until stool or urine confirms infection

steps.title: Acute Weakness
steps.text: In which scenario is a lumbar puncture contraindicated in a child with acute weakness?
steps.rationale: The guideline notes that LP is contraindicated in children with signs of raised ICP or compressive spinal pathology.

textChoices.text: Fever and neck stiffness
History of convulsions
Meningitis suspected without rash
Signs of raised intracranial pressure or suspected compressive myelopathy

steps.title: Acute Weakness
steps.text: What is the immediate management for a suspected cerebral or spinal abscess in a child?
steps.rationale: Management of cerebral or spinal abscess should include empiric antibiotics (like Ceftriaxone) and urgent surgical consultation.

textChoices.text: Start Ceftriaxone and seek surgical opinion
Begin TB treatment and high-dose steroids
Wait for culture results before starting any treatment
Perform LP immediately

steps.title: Acute Weakness
steps.text: What is the recommended action for a spinal cord lesion not caused by bacterial infection?
steps.rationale: The guideline advises that steroids should be considered for all spinal cord lesions, unless bacterial infection is present.

textChoices.text: Delay all medications until cause is confirmed
Start high-dose antibiotics immediately
Consider steroid therapy unless bacterial infection is suspected
Refer only if there is limb paralysis

steps.title: Acute Weakness
steps.text: Which of the following is the correct management for TB spine or tuberculoma presenting with acute weakness?
steps.rationale: For TB spine and tuberculoma, the guideline recommends initiating TB therapy and seeking orthopaedic input.

textChoices.text: Immediate LP and referral
IV steroids only
Surgical decompression in all cases
Start TB treatment and consult orthopaedics

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: Which of the following is essential in the acute management of a stroke patient?
steps.rationale: Monitoring fluid balance is vital to avoid both dehydration and fluid overload, especially in neurocritical care.

textChoices.text: Maintain hyperglycaemia to preserve brain function
Limit monitoring to twice daily assessments
Avoid fluid administration entirely
Monitor fluid input and output carefully

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: In haemorrhagic stroke, what is the appropriate approach to managing elevated blood pressure?
steps.rationale: Antihypertensives must be used carefully to avoid hypoperfusion and maintain cerebral blood flow.

textChoices.text: Aggressively lower BP with high-dose diuretics
Ignore BP unless it exceeds 200 mmHg
Stop all medications that affect BP
Use antihypertensives cautiously with frequent BP monitoring

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: What is one of the earliest signs of raised intracranial pressure in a conscious child?
steps.rationale: Vomiting and headache, particularly if worsening or occurring with position changes, can indicate rising ICP.

textChoices.text: Frequent urination
Vomiting and worsening headache
Dry skin
Decreased appetite

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: Which of the following interventions helps reduce raised intracranial pressure (ICP)?
steps.rationale: Elevating the head improves venous return and lowers ICP—an essential neuroprotective step.

textChoices.text: Encouraging supine positioning
Starting antibiotics
Increasing oral fluid intake
Elevating the head of the bed to at least 30 degrees

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: In ischaemic stroke in children, what determines the decision to start aspirin?
steps.rationale: Aspirin may be used in ischaemic stroke only after senior consultation and assessment for bleeding risk.

textChoices.text: MRI scan results
Discussion with consultant and absence of haemorrhagic risk
Age of the child
Presence of fever

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: What is an appropriate component of supportive stroke care in a high-dependency setting?
steps.rationale: Hourly turning reduces risk of pressure injuries, an important part of stroke nursing care.

textChoices.text: Encourage complete bed rest for days
Avoid turning to prevent dislodging IV lines
Limit nutrition until full recovery
Turn hourly to prevent pressure sores

steps.title: Stroke
steps.text: What is recommended as part of long-term stroke management in children?
steps.rationale: Early and continued rehabilitation, including physiotherapy, is critical for maximizing recovery.

textChoices.text: Discontinue therapy once the child is stable
Continue physiotherapy tailored to neurological severity
Avoid involving caregivers to reduce burden
Restrict activity to prevent further strokes

steps.title: Hydrocephalus
steps.text: What is an important consideration when treating hydrocephalus with ventriculitis at secondary or tertiary level?
steps.rationale: The guideline notes that ventriculitis may require prolonged parenteral therapy, which can extend beyond typical meningitis treatment durations.

textChoices.text: It may require longer parenteral treatment than typical meningitis
Oral antibiotics are sufficient in most cases
Treat only after surgical intervention
Avoid antibiotics until CSF is drained

steps.title: Hydrocephalus
steps.text: What is the recommended emergency intervention for a child with acute raised intracranial pressure (ICP) due to hydrocephalus?
steps.rationale: A therapeutic ventricular tap is advised to relieve acute raised ICP while awaiting definitive treatment.

textChoices.text: Administer high-dose steroids and observe
Order urgent CT and delay intervention
Perform a therapeutic ventricular tap
Refer only if symptoms persist after 48 hours

steps.title: Hydrocephalus
steps.text: While awaiting surgery in a child with communicating hydrocephalus, what medical treatment can help reduce CSF production?
steps.rationale: Acetazolamide or Furosemide can be used to temporarily reduce CSF production in communicating hydrocephalus.

textChoices.text: Paracetamol and bed rest
Mannitol infusion
Broad-spectrum antibiotics
Acetazolamide or Furosemide

steps.title: Hydrocephalus
steps.text: What is the definitive treatment option for hydrocephalus at tertiary level?
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends ventriculo-peritoneal (VP) shunt or endoscopic third ventriculostomy (ETV) as the definitive surgical treatments for hydrocephalus.

textChoices.text: Repeat doses of diuretics
Neurosurgical referral for VP shunt or ETV
Monitoring with supportive care only
Intermittent ventricular taps

steps.title: Blocked Shunts
steps.text: What is the correct initial step in managing a child with a suspected blocked VP shunt at secondary care?
steps.rationale: The guideline advises that secondary-level providers assess the child and initiate ABCD management as part of stabilizing care.

textChoices.text: Observe the child overnight and reassess
Assess the patient and initiate ABCD management
Start oral antibiotics and refer within 72 hours
Perform a lumbar puncture for CSF analysis

steps.title: Blocked Shunts
steps.text: What is the appropriate antibiotic management for a suspected blocked shunt with infection at secondary level?
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends starting IV Ceftriaxone as for meningitis before urgent referral to a tertiary facility.

textChoices.text: Oral amoxicillin at 40 mg/kg twice daily
Topical antibiotics with wound care
Delay antibiotics until referral is confirmed
Start IV Ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg daily, then refer

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: What is the purpose of visual and hearing assessments in children with cerebral palsy at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: Visual and hearing issues can significantly affect communication, learning, and safety, so early assessment and intervention are key.

textChoices.text: To determine if sedation is needed during therapy
To detect impairments that may impact development and communication
To prepare the child for orthopedic surgery
To evaluate readiness for boarding school

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: Which therapy is specifically recommended for improving mobility and chest health in children with cerebral palsy?
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends physiotherapy for limb function, chest care, and support with mobility aids like wheelchairs.

textChoices.text: Occupational therapy
Physiotherapy
Cognitive behavioral therapy
Nutritional rehabilitation

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: What role does speech therapy play in the care of a child with cerebral palsy at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: Speech therapy is crucial not only for communication, but also for feeding difficulties, such as impaired sucking and swallowing.

textChoices.text: Language tutoring for non-verbal children
Correction of hearing loss
Assessment and therapy for sucking and swallowing difficulties
Management of seizures

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: Which medication is recommended as an initial antispasmodic treatment for children with CP?
steps.rationale: The guideline recommends Diazepam 1–2.5 mg orally every 4–6 hoursas a first-line treatment for spasticity, with dose adjustment based on the child’s response.

textChoices.text: Ibuprofen
Diazepam
Amoxicillin
Paracetamol

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: When should a child with cerebral palsy and epilepsy be referred to a central hospital?
steps.rationale: Children with poorly controlled epilepsy or in need of specialist services not available at district level should be referred to tertiary care.

textChoices.text: After one or two isolated seizures that resolved without treatment
If the child shows developmental delay but no seizures are present
When epilepsy is poorly controlled or specialist services are not available locally
Only after completing a full course of antiseizure medication without improvement

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: What is an important responsibility of secondary-level facilities in coordinating care for children with CP?
steps.rationale: The guideline stresses coordinated care through general clinics and involvement of various specialties to manage CP comprehensively.

textChoices.text: Managing care in isolation to simplify follow-up
Coordinating multidisciplinary follow-up and clinic care
Prioritizing home care over clinic reviews
Referring all cases to private specialists

steps.title: Cerebral Palsy (CP)
steps.text: What should be the approach to epilepsy in children with cerebral palsy at the secondary care level?
steps.rationale: The guideline clearly states that seizure management should begin in all children with epilepsy, rather than waiting for tertiary evaluation.

textChoices.text: Observe seizures unless they occur more than twice weekly
Refer immediately without starting any medication
Initiate seizure management in all children with epilepsy
Avoid treatment until EEG confirmation is available

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: Which of the following is an indication to initiate long-term anticonvulsant therapy in a child?
steps.rationale: Long-term treatment is initiated if the child has had more than 2 afebrile seizures, or 1 afebrile seizure with a neurological condition.

textChoices.text: One febrile seizure without neurological concerns
A single afebrile seizure in a healthy child
Two or more afebrile seizures
Febrile seizure in a child with a viral illness

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: What is the first-line antiepileptic treatment for focal seizures in children?
steps.rationale: Carbamazepine and Valproate are first-line choices for focal seizures according to the guideline.

textChoices.text: Carbamazepine or Valproate
Lamotrigine or Phenytoin
Levetiracetam or Phenobarbitone
Valproate or Ethosuximide

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: For a child with absence seizures, which antiepileptic drug is considered first-line?
steps.rationale: Valproate and Ethosuximide are the first-line drugs for absence seizures. Carbamazepine can worsen this type.

textChoices.text: Carbamazepine
Levetiracetam
Valproate
Phenytoin

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: What is the recommended approach for antiepileptic medication dosing in children?
steps.rationale: Monotherapy is preferred, starting at the minimum therapeutic dose and titrating upwards to avoid side effects.

textChoices.text: Combine two drugs from the beginning
Start at maximum dose and reduce as needed
Start at minimum dose and titrate upwards
Base dosing solely on age

steps.title: Epilepsy
steps.text: When should a child on antiepileptic therapy be referred to a tertiary facility?
steps.rationale: Complex cases with poor seizure control despite two antiepileptic drugs at optimal doses should be referred for specialist care and further evaluation.

textChoices.text: After the second seizure
If the first-line medication causes mild nausea
After one month of therapy
If seizure control is not attained after 3 months on 2 drugs at optimal doses

steps.title: Neural Tube Defects
steps.text: A newborn with a neural tube defect arrives at a secondary care facility. What is the correct immediate nursing position for the baby?
steps.rationale: Prone positioning helps protect the defect and prevents rupture or infection.

textChoices.text: Prone with careful protection of the defect
Supine with a head elevation
On the side with legs extended
Sitting in an upright infant seat

steps.title: Neural Tube Defects
steps.text: What is the appropriate wound care for a leaking NTD lesion at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: Vaseline gauze keeps the area moist and antibiotics help prevent infection. Aminoglycosides like gentamicin should be avoided.

textChoices.text: Irrigate with saline and apply a plaster dressing
Apply betadine and leave uncovered
Cover with vaseline gauze and start Ceftriaxone and Metronidazole
Cover with dry gauze and give gentamicin

steps.title: Neural Tube Defects
steps.text: Which of the following is an indication for urgent urological review in a baby with NTD?
steps.rationale: Retention or failure to pass urine may indicate neurogenic bladder and requires early urological input.

textChoices.text: The baby has a mild fever
The baby is feeding poorly
The baby is crying excessively
The baby is not passing urine or is in retention

steps.title: Neural Tube Defects
steps.text: Why is orthopedic evaluation important in the management of NTD at the secondary level?
steps.rationale: Limb deformities such as genu varus/valgus are common and require orthopedic assessment.

textChoices.text: To correct associated deformities like genu varus or valgus
To prescribe physical therapy exercises
To assess fine motor skills
To check for scoliosis

steps.title: Neural Tube Defects
steps.text: Which of the following statements is TRUE about referral to tertiary care for a baby with NTD?
steps.rationale: Stable babies can be referred the next day. Night travel is not required unless the baby is unstable.

textChoices.text: Referral should be avoided if the lesion is closed
Referral is only necessary if the baby is symptomatic
The baby should be urgently transported at night
Referral can wait until the next day if the baby is stable

steps.title: Headache
steps.text: Which of the following is a recommended strategy when managing recurrent headaches in children?
steps.rationale: Keeping a detailed diary helps identify headache patterns and triggers, which is a foundational step in headache management.

textChoices.text: Start medication immediately without diary tracking
Keep a diary recording quality, severity, timing, and triggers
Advise complete school absence until headache-free
Avoid all physical activity for at least one week

steps.title: Headache
steps.text: What advice should be given regarding school attendance for children with recurrent headaches?
steps.rationale: Children should return to school, and short breaks (e.g., 15 minutes in the nurse’s office during peak headache) are acceptable to help them cope.

textChoices.text: Keep them home until headaches fully resolve
Encourage return to school with planned breaks if needed
Restrict school participation to half-days permanently
Recommend homeschooling due to stress factors

steps.title: Headache
steps.text: Which of the following is not a recommended lifestyle modification for children with recurrent headaches?
steps.rationale: Overuse of analgesics can worsen headaches; medication overuse is a known risk factor for chronic headaches.

textChoices.text: Avoiding headache triggers like caffeine and poor sleep
Daily physical exercise
Using painkillers daily to prevent headache onset
Managing comorbid sleep problems

steps.title: Headache
steps.text: What is an important goal of patient education in headache management?
steps.rationale: Empowering patients enhances self-control and can significantly reduce distress and improve outcomes in chronic headache management.

textChoices.text: Minimize the use of diaries and self-monitoring
Focus solely on pharmacological solutions
Enable patients to manage their disease and reduce psychological distress
Encourage complete avoidance of social interaction
